perturbation.original_text: then the Manager shall curtail the Projects of the LIFO Partners. Nothing contained in the preceding sentence shall affect the rights of the Manager to curtail or disconnect the Project of a Partner under Section 4 of that Partner's Technical Use Agreement. Any curtailment under this provision shall be in an amount sufficient to cause the Transmission Line to deliver power at its designed capacity, availability and voltage, or to reduce the Transmission Line line losses, as applicable, up to and including the Projects of all of the LIFO Partners.
perturbation.changed_text: then the Manager may consider curtailing the Projects of the LIFO Partners if deemed necessary. Nothing contained in the preceding sentence shall affect the rights of the Manager to curtail or disconnect the Project of a Partner under Section 4 of that Partner's Technical Use Agreement. Any curtailment under this provision should be in an amount that seems appropriate to influence the Transmission Line to deliver power near its designed capacity, availability and voltage, or attempt to reduce the Transmission Line line losses, as applicable, up to the Projects of all of the LIFO Partners.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall curtail' to 'may consider curtailing if deemed necessary' introduces ambiguity and discretion where a mandatory action was previously required. Replacing 'shall be in an amount sufficient' with 'should be in an amount that seems appropriate to influence' makes the required curtailment level subjective and unenforceable. This weakening of the obligation can lead to disputes over whether the manager has fulfilled their responsibilities.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Public Utilities Code Section 451 requires that public utilities' charges, practices, equipment, and services shall be just and reasonable. The original text ensured a mandatory curtailment under specific conditions to maintain the Transmission Line's designed capacity and voltage, which aligns with providing reasonable service. The modified text, by making the curtailment discretionary and the amount subjective, could result in the Manager not taking sufficient action, potentially leading to unreliable service and therefore violating the requirement for just and reasonable service.

REGULATION OF PUBLIC UTILITIES [201 - 3297] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1951, Ch. 764. ) PART 1. PUBLIC UTILITIES ACT [201 - 2120] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1951, Ch. 764. ) CHAPTER 3. Rights and Obligations of Public Utilities [451 - 666] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1951, Ch. 764. ) ARTICLE 1. Rates [451 - 468] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1951, Ch. 764. ) 451. All charges demanded or received by any public utility, or by any two or more public utilities, for any product or commodity furnished or to be furnished or any service rendered or to be rendered shall be just and reasonable. Every unjust or unreasonable charge demanded or received for such product or commodity or service is unlawful. Every public utility shall furnish and maintain such adequate, efficient, just, and reasonable service, instrumentalities, equipment, and facilities, including telephone facilities, as defined in Section 54.1 of the Civil Code, as are necessary to promote the safety, health, comfort, and convenience of its patrons, employees, and the public. All rules made by a public utility affecting or pertaining to its charges or service to the public shall be just and reasonable. (Amended by Stats. 1977, Ch. 700.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity by changing 'shall curtail' to 'may consider curtailing if deemed necessary,' and 'shall be in an amount sufficient' to 'should be in an amount that seems appropriate to influence.' This weakens the original obligation. PUC 451 states that every public utility 'shall furnish and maintain such adequate, efficient, just, and reasonable service, instrumentalities, equipment, and facilities...as are necessary to promote the safety, health, comfort, and convenience of its patrons, employees, and the public.' By making the curtailment discretionary and the amount subjective, the modified text could result in the Manager not taking sufficient action, potentially leading to unreliable service and therefore violating the requirement for just and reasonable service, instrumentalities, equipment, and facilities.

perturbation.original_text: Such curtailment shall continue until the Manager shall determine that reconnecting the Projects of the LIFO Partners shall not result in the recurrence of the event giving rise to the curtailment.
perturbation.changed_text: Such curtailment may continue as long as the Manager feels it is necessary to prevent potential issues.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a condition for ending the curtailment (when the manager determines reconnection won't cause recurrence). The modified text replaces this with a vague, subjective standard ('as long as the Manager feels it is necessary to prevent potential issues'). This ambiguity allows the manager to continue curtailment indefinitely based on their subjective feeling, even if objective conditions suggest it's no longer needed.
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing curtailment to continue indefinitely based on the manager's subjective feeling ('as long as the Manager feels it is necessary to prevent potential issues'), the modified text could lead to unjust and unreasonable service for the LIFO partners, violating California Public Utilities Code Section 451. The LIFO Partners' projects could be curtailed without a clear, objective justification, which is not just and reasonable.

REGULATION OF PUBLIC UTILITIES [201 - 3297] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1951, Ch. 764. ) PART 1. PUBLIC UTILITIES ACT [201 - 2120] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1951, Ch. 764. ) CHAPTER 3. Rights and Obligations of Public Utilities [451 - 666] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1951, Ch. 764. ) ARTICLE 1. Rates [451 - 468] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1951, Ch. 764. ) 451. All charges demanded or received by any public utility, or by any two or more public utilities, for any product or commodity furnished or to be furnished or any service rendered or to be rendered shall be just and reasonable. Every unjust or unreasonable charge demanded or received for such product or commodity or service is unlawful. Every public utility shall furnish and maintain such adequate, efficient, just, and reasonable service, instrumentalities, equipment, and facilities, including telephone facilities, as defined in Section 54.1 of the Civil Code, as are necessary to promote the safety, health, comfort, and convenience of its patrons, employees, and the public. All rules made by a public utility affecting or pertaining to its charges or service to the public shall be just and reasonable. (Amended by Stats. 1977, Ch. 700.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Public Utilities Code Section 451. The original text provided a specific condition for ending curtailment. The changed text replaces this with a subjective standard, allowing curtailment to continue as long as the manager 'feels it is necessary,' even if objective conditions suggest otherwise. This contradicts PUC 451, which requires that all service provided by a public utility be 'just and reasonable.' By allowing curtailment based solely on the manager's subjective feeling, the modified text creates a situation where service may not be 'just and reasonable,' as the curtailment could continue indefinitely without clear justification, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: If the LIFO Partners shall inform the Manager in writing of an agreement among them concerning the priority of the curtailment of their respective Projects, the Manager shall effect any curtailment under these provisions according to such priority. If the LIFO Partners fail to so inform the Manager, the Manager shall in its discretion determine which of the Projects of the LIFO Partners to curtail.
perturbation.changed_text: If the LIFO Partners inform the Manager of a potential agreement concerning the curtailment priorities, the Manager will consider their input. If no clear guidance is provided, the Manager will decide as they see fit.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes a clear protocol: If the LIFO Partners agree on curtailment priority, the Manager *shall* follow it. If they don't, the Manager has discretion. The modified text weakens this significantly. 'Inform the Manager of a potential agreement' is less binding than a written agreement. 'Will consider their input' is far weaker than 'shall effect any curtailment.' 'Decide as they see fit' removes any obligation to consider the LIFO Partners' interests, even if they provide input. This creates ambiguity about the extent to which the Manager must consider the LIFO Partners' agreed-upon priorities or even their individual project needs.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text established a clear process for curtailment prioritization that provided some protection for the LIFO Partners' projects. By replacing mandatory adherence to their agreement with a vague 'will consider their input,' the modified text opens the door for arbitrary curtailment decisions that could disproportionately and unfairly harm certain LIFO Partners. This could be argued as providing unjust and unreasonable service in violation of California Public Utilities Code Section 451, as the Manager could ignore reasonable prioritization requests and curtail projects based on factors unrelated to grid stability or equitable treatment.

REGULATION OF PUBLIC UTILITIES [201 - 3297] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1951, Ch. 764. ) PART 1. PUBLIC UTILITIES ACT [201 - 2120] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1951, Ch. 764. ) CHAPTER 3. Rights and Obligations of Public Utilities [451 - 666] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1951, Ch. 764. ) ARTICLE 1. Rates [451 - 468] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1951, Ch. 764. ) 451. All charges demanded or received by any public utility, or by any two or more public utilities, for any product or commodity furnished or to be furnished or any service rendered or to be rendered shall be just and reasonable. Every unjust or unreasonable charge demanded or received for such product or commodity or service is unlawful. Every public utility shall furnish and maintain such adequate, efficient, just, and reasonable service, instrumentalities, equipment, and facilities, including telephone facilities, as defined in Section 54.1 of the Civil Code, as are necessary to promote the safety, health, comfort, and convenience of its patrons, employees, and the public. All rules made by a public utility affecting or pertaining to its charges or service to the public shall be just and reasonable. (Amended by Stats. 1977, Ch. 700.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the original agreement regarding curtailment priorities, potentially contradicting the requirement for 'just and reasonable service' as mandated by California Public Utilities Code Section 451. The original text required the Manager to effect curtailment according to the LIFO Partners' agreement. The modified text replaces this mandatory obligation with a weaker 'will consider their input,' potentially allowing the Manager to disregard the partners' agreement and make arbitrary decisions. Snippet 1 states that every public utility shall furnish 'just and reasonable service'. By allowing the manager to arbitrarily curtail projects without adhering to the agreed upon priority, the manager is not providing 'just and reasonable service'.
